C1.notes: Rubric says 'ABI-compatible with tree-sitter 0.26.6', implying the expected finding is compatibility. Pilot-02 conclusively found INcompatibility (>=0.21,<0.23 vs workspace 0.26.x, no overlap) yet was scored 1 with justification 'fully resolves the ABI compatibility question'. Pilot-01 deferred to a smoke-test and was scored 0. The scorer applied a 'fully characterized' standard for pilot-02 but a 'resolved' standard for pilot-01, which are asymmetric. Under a strict reading of the rubric the correct finding (incompatible) should score 0, not 1. The rubric text needs clarification before the full run to prevent scorer variance across 10 runs.

C2.notes: Pilot-01 raw JSON explicitly states 'Companion objects use object_declaration with companion keyword modifier' in library_findings; score 1 is faithful. Pilot-02 library_findings and approaches enumerate object_declaration but never mention companion objects or the keyword modifier distinction; score 0 is faithful.

C5.notes: Rubric requires 'at least 1 test with .kts syntax'. Pilot-01 test_coverage lists 11 behaviors all targeting .kt scenarios; no .kts test anywhere in the JSON. Pilot-02 test_coverage proposes two tests (element_query_happy_path, extract_inheritance) with no .kts variant. Both scores of 0 are faithful to the criterion.

C7.notes: Rubric requires 'feature flag added to default'. Pilot-01 constraints section never states lang-kotlin must be in the default feature set; score 0 is faithful. Pilot-02 constraints section explicitly says 'the lang-kotlin feature must be included in the default feature set'; score 1 is faithful.

ceiling_floor.rationale: Pilot scores of 4 and 5 sit in the middle of the 0-7 range. C5 scored 0 in both pilots and is the hardest criterion (requires explicit .kts test design); this acts as a natural ceiling brake. C3, C4, and C6 appear reliably achievable from standard crate research, preventing floor collapse. The rubric pilot-gate threshold (6/7 in both pilots) was not reached, confirming no ceiling. Floor is equally unlikely given that three criteria (C3, C4, C6) require only pattern recognition from existing code, not novel synthesis.

statistical_power.minimum_detectable_delta: ~3 points on a 0-7 scale (Mann-Whitney U critical value ≤ 2 with n=5 per group, two-tailed alpha=0.05, requires near-complete rank separation: 23+ of 25 pairwise treatment-beats-control comparisons)
statistical_power.notes: A median delta of 1 from two pilots is well below the minimum detectable effect. For U ≤ 2, every treatment score must exceed every control score or be separated by only a single inversion. With scores clustering around 4-5 and a 0-7 range, the groups would need to be almost entirely non-overlapping. Protocol pre-acknowledges under-power; a null result at n=5 cannot rule out a real effect of 1-2 points.

blockers: C1 rubric phrasing is ambiguous and was scored inconsistently: 'ABI-compatible' can mean 'confirm it is compatible' (strict) or 'characterize compatibility status' (loose). Pilot-02 was rewarded for finding INcompatibility, which contradicts the strict reading. If the full-run scorer applies different interpretations across runs, C1 variance will be scorer noise rather than treatment signal. The rubric must be clarified before any full run is spawned.
recommendations: Rewrite C1 as: 'ABI relationship between tree-sitter-kotlin 0.3.8 and tree-sitter 0.26.6 fully characterized with evidence (semver ranges or crate source); incompatibility is a valid finding if documented.' This removes the ambiguity between 'confirm compatible' and 'characterize status' and aligns with what a good scout actually produces.

Add a scoring note to C5: 'Listing .kts in EXTENSION_MAP is insufficient; a distinct test case parsing .kts syntax (e.g. a top-level or extension function) must be present in the test plan.' Both pilots demonstrated awareness of .kts support but still omitted the test; the scoring boundary needs to be explicit.

Accept that the experiment is under-powered: document in the final report that a non-significant Mann-Whitney result is expected even if a real 1-point effect exists. The pilot gate passes (neither pilot hit 6/7), so proceed with the full run, but treat statistical significance as a secondary indicator.
summary: The pilot gate passes cleanly (4/7 and 5/7, well below the 6/7 ceiling threshold) and rubric discrimination is adequate for criteria C2-C6. The single blocking concern is C1: the rubric assumes ABI compatibility is the expected finding, but the correct research outcome is incompatibility, and the two pilot scores were assigned under different implicit standards. This ambiguity must be resolved in the rubric before the full run to prevent scorer variance from contaminating the treatment signal. Statistical power is low by design and acknowledged in the protocol; the experiment can proceed with that caveat.
